def: n. f. 1. Loss and gain. Hār jīt qismat kī hai . Prov. Gain and loss are fate's decree. 2. Gambling; hazard. Kisī ne ghar kī havelī girau rakhā hārī, Jo kuchh thī jins muyassar banā banā hārī, Kisī ne chīz kisī kī churā chhupā hārī, Kisī ne gaṭhṛī paṛosan kī apnī lā hārī, Yeh hār-jīt kā charchā paṛā Divālī meṅ . Nazīr. Some mortgaged their own houses which they lost at play, What goods lay to their hands they gambled straight away, Some lost what they did filch and stealthily convey, Some stole and lost their neighbour's bundles, sooth to say, Such thefts and gambling mark this feast of lights today! hār-jīt k . To bet; gamble. hār-kar, hāre ke darje, hāre-darje , adv. 1. Forcibly; compulsorily. 2. Nolens volens ; unwillingly. 3. At last. hār mānnā , v. n. 1. To acknowledge oneself beaten. 2. To submit, or bow to.

v. n. S. हारण 1. To fail; lose. 1. Khele so hāre . Prov. Who plays loses. 2. Sat hārā, aur gayā mārā . Prov. Your courage fled you're beaten dead. 3. Hāre juārī ko kab kal paṛtī hai? To lose one's courage or spirits, to be discouraged. 4. To be defeated, overcome. Āp hāre, bahū ko māre . Prov. His money he loses, his wife he bruises. 5. def: s.m. Name of an angel (he, together with another, named Mārūt, severely censured mankind before the throne of God; they were, therefore, both sent down to earth in human shapes to judge of the temptations to which man was subject. They could not withstand them; they were seduced by women, and committed every species of iniquity, for which they were suspended by the feet in a well in Babylon, where they are to remain in great torment till the day of judgment. — See the Qorʼān, Sūra 2; Sale's Transl. p. 20).
more: n. prop. The name of an angel, who, together with another named ماروت mārūt, severely censured mankind before the throne of God: they were, therefore, both sent down to earth in human shapes, to judge of the temptations to which man was subject, and they could not withstand them; being seduced by the planet Venus in form of a woman: for which they were suspended by the feet in a well in Babylon, where they are to remain in great torment till the day of judgment. ( Ḳurʼān, Sūrat 2. See Maracci. p. 44. Sale, Vol. I. p. 20).

more: n. f. حسر taken off, stripped. Keen, poignant regret. 1. Khil-ke gul kuchh to bahār apnī, sabā, dikhlā gaë, Hasrat un gunchoṅ pe hai jo bin khile murjhā gaë! Some flowers their ripened bloom, O wanton breeze! just showed and died, Alas! for those which died before their spring they yet enjoyed! 2. Uskī to vahāṅ ḍholak o mirdang bajegī, Aur rūh terī qabr meṅ hasrat se jalegī . The miser's estate. Nazīr. While drums for them their joyous music make, Your heart even in your grave will burn and ache. 2. Wish; desire ( ārzū ). Āṅkheṅ merī talvoṅ se woh mal jāë to achchhā, Hai hasrat-i-pābos nikal jāë to achchhā . Zauq. My eyes I'd pluck, so 'neath her heels they might crushed be, Her feet kissed and my heart content, my life may flee!

(from حسر Heb. Defect, want, privation) s. f. Regret, grief, desire. حسرت آلوده ḥasrat-ālūda, adj. Overwhelmed with grief. حسرت زده ḥasrat-zada, adj. Wobegone, afflicted. حسرت کشته ḥasrat-kushta, Killed with grief.

def: adj. & s.m. Endued with the five senses; acute, shrewd; — an animal.
more: adj. (superl. from حسّ) 1. Possessed of the five senses. 2. (used substantively) m. An animal.

ety: v.n. fr. hastan, rt. Zend ah = S. अस्
def: s.f. Existence, being (i.q. hastī); — (pres. tense, 3rd pers. sing. of hastan) is, exists
more: s. m. 1. The hand. 2. A cubit.
s. m. (dakh.) Elephant: see هستي hastī.
s. m. Name of the 13th mansion of the moon. (Corvus).
subs: or hast-būd, s.f.(?) 'What is and was'; a calculation of the data of the present and past; present actual state (of revenue, or income) compared with former years; — an estimate of the assets of a tract of land; — rent-roll; — (corr. of hast-nā-būd) a remission granted by zamīndārs for the portion of land failing in produce.

def: s.f. The thirteenth lunar asterism (represented by a hand, and containing five stars; identified by some with part of the constellation Corvus).
more: s. m. Name of the 13th mansion of the moon. (Corvus).

def: part. (of hasnā), Laughing, smiling (i.q. haṅstā).

s.m. The hand; — the thirteenth lunar asterism (i.q. hastā, q.v.); — the fore-arm; a cubit (measured from the elbow to the tip of the middle finger); — an elephant's trunk; — (S. hastam) a pair of bellows
subs: vulg. hastāććhar (˚ta+ak˚), s.m. One's own sign-manual, autograph, signature, handwriting
s.f. Deeds of the hand, handiwork, any manual performance.

def: n. f. 1. Being; existence. 2. The world; life. Adam se jānib-i-hastī talāsh-i-yār meṅ āë . From non-existence to this world of being in search of our beloved we come. 3. Worth; merit ( asal , 7). Abe! terī hastī kyā hai? What is your origin, fellow?

ety: hast, q.v.+ī = Zend i = S. इ
def: s.f. Being, existence; entity; world; life; — wealth, riches; — worth, merit.
ex:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری
صَدِیوں رَہَا ہَے دُشْمَن دَورِ زَماں ہَمارا
kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī
sadiyõ rahā hai duśman daur-i zamā̃ hamārā

There is something about our existence for it doesn't get wiped
Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَسْتِی (hastī). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi ہستی (hsty).

def: s.m. An elephant; — (hastī) name of an ancient king of the Lunar race (founder of the city of Hastinā-pur)
subs: s.m. Tusk of an elephant; ivory (i.q. hāthī-dāṅt)
s.m. The ichor or exudation from an elephant's temples when in rut.
ex:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری

kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī

Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.

def: adj. (f. -ī), Relating or belonging to elephants; elephantine, as big as an elephant, huge; — s.m. The city of Hastināpur

s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.

s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.
more: s. m. Ancient Dihlī, the capital of Yudhish̤ṭhira and his brethren, the remains of which still exist, about 57 miles N. E. of the modern city, on the banks of the old channel of the Ganges.

def: s.f. A female elephant (i.q. hathnī, q.v.); — the lowest and worst of the four classes into which women are divided; — a woman of that class (see padminī).
more: s. f. 1. A description of woman, the worst of the four classes: see under پدمني. 2. A female elephant.

def: s.m. = hastinā-pur, q.v. s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.
direct plural of ہَسْتی (hastī, “existence; being”)
adj. Of or belonging to an elephant.
oblique plural of ہَسْتی (hastī, “existence; being”)
vocative plural of ہَسْتی (hastī, “existence; being”)
n. f. lit . a female elephant. The lowest and worst of the four classes into which women are divided.
adj. Belonging to the hand; done with the hand, manual; given with the hand.
adj. Envious, spiteful, malignant.
more: (from حسد) adj. Envious, malignant. ¶ ḥusūd, s. Envying, envy.

def: adj. (f. -ā), To be laughed at, laughable, ridiculous; — (S. hāsyam), s.m. Laughter, laughing, mirth (i.q. haṅsī); jest; amusement
subs: s.m. The sentiment of humour; sense of humour, the comic vein, facetiousness.

def: n. f. S. हट्ट 1. A shop ( dūkān ). Nir bhāgī ne kholī hāṭ, le gaë chor bharat or bāt . Prov. A luckless fellow did a shop set up, with all his goods and weights a thief made off. 2. A mart. 3. A market day ( peṅṭh ). hāṭ k. , v. n. 1. To open a shop. 2. To market. hāṭ kholnā , To open a shop.
s.m. (dialec. or colloq.) = hāth, q.v.
more: s. m. The hand: see هاتهہ hāth. هات سون جانا hāt sūṅ jānā (dakh. i. q. بي اختيارهونا) To be helpless or not knowing what to do. هات کاٿنا hāt kāṭnā (dakh. i. q. افسوس کرنا) To sigh, to lament. هات مين لاپات مين دهرچاٿنا hāt meṅ lā pāt meṅ dhar chāṭnā (dakh. met. for اسراف کرنا) To be profuse, to practise extravagance. هات مون کا hāt mūṅ kā (dakh.) See سرمايه sar-māya.
subs: s.m. (Dakh.) Means, resources, capital, stock (syn. sar-māya)

def: s.f. A market, a mart; a movable market, a fair; — a shop, a stall; — a market-day (syn. peṅṭh)
more: s. f. A market, a moveable market or fair, a shop.
subs: v.n. To go to market, to market; — to open a shop; to keep a stall
wety: First attested in c. 1780 as Middle Hindi ہاٹ (haṭ) and Old Hindi हाट (hāṭa) in c. 1420, inherited from Prakrit 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝 m (haṭṭa), 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀻 f (haṭṭī), from Sanskrit हट्ट m (haṭṭa, “market; fair”), हट्टी f (haṭṭī, “small market or fair”). Cognate with Bengali হাট (haṭ), Gujarati હાટ (hāṭ), Punjabi ہٹ / ਹੱਟ (haṭṭ), and Sindhi هَٽُ (haṭu).

def: intj. Begone! away!
more: n. m. S. हित 1. True affection. 1. Ghar kī nār meṅ bahut hit, jā soṅ rahai sadā saṅg lāgī, Jab yeh hans taje yeh kāyā 'piret piret' kar bhāge . Kabīr. The wife affection great doth show, close to her spouse she lies, But when his soul his body quits 'the de'il' she eries and flies. (When the body dies it is believed to be possessed by an evil spirit, and no one will approach it). 2. Kār hit kā or biyāh bit kā . Rus. Work with heart, marry when you 've money. 2. def: n. m. S. हस्त Pr. hattho ; A. yad . 1. The hand; arm. 1. Hāth ko hāth paichhāntā hai . Prov. Hand knoweth hand. (The creditor knows his debtor). 2. Lāṭhī hāth kī, bhāī sāth kā . Prov. A club in the hand, a brother nigh. 3. Maulā hāth baṛhāiāṅ, jis chāheṅ tis deṅ . Prov. Advancement comes from God, he gives to whom He wills. 4. Āge hāth, pīchhe pāt . Prov. Before her her hands, behind but leaves. (She is so poor that she has to cover her pudendum with her hands and her back with leaves). 5. Hān, lābh, jīvan, maran, jas, apjas Bidhi hāth . Prov. Loss and gain, life and death, honor and disgrace in God's hands are. 6. Bāman se bāman mile kar-kar lambe hāth, Den len ko kuchh nahīṅ, namaskār kī bāt . When Brahmans meet hands are stretched free, Transactions none, mere courtesy. 7. Hāth māṅ nā gāt māṅ, 'main dhanvantī jāt māṅ .' Prov. Not a rap she has got, no jewels her grace: 'I'm of a wealthy high-born race.' 8. Hathiyā chale na paīyāṅ, baiṭhe de, Gusaiyāṅ! Prov. Nor hand nor foot can I e'en move, my food send me, O God above! 2. A cubit measure; half a yard. Do hāth kā ek gaz . 3. A hand (in an Office, etc.). Āj kal hāth kam ho gaë haiṅ . (Artizans). 4. Reach. 5. Possession; clutches. 6. Protection; patronage. 7. Power. Ab tak to yeh bāt hamāre hāth meṅ hai . 8. A hand in cards. hāth, hāthoṅ , adv. By the hands of; through. hāth utarnā , v. n. To be dislocated (one's hand). hāth uṭhā-ke denā , v. a. 1. To give with one's hand. 2. To give reluctantly. hāth uṭhā-ke kosnā . To call down curses on one. Hāth uṭhā-kar woh kosne jo lage, Wāh re! ham use duā samjhe! Mukhaīyar. With hands upraised she called down curses on my head, How excellent! for blessings I took them instead. hāth uṭhānā , v. n. 1. To raise or lift up one's hands; to salute. 2. To lift up the hands in prayer; to pray for one. 3. To lift one's hands against one. Bīvī par hāth uṭhātā hai . 4. ( hāth dhonā ) To wash one's hands of; to keep from; desist; abstain; refrain or withdraw from; resign ( dast-bardar h .). Hāth uṭhā jaur aur jafā se tū! Ye hī goyā salām hai terā . Your hands from tyranny withdraw, this for your salutation I'll receive. 5. To give up hope; to despair. hāth ānā, paṛnā, lagnā, yā chaṛhnā, hāth meṅ ānā ; Sant. hatāo , 1. To come to hand; fall to one; come into one's possession or power. 1. Hāth lag-jānā shart hai . Provided I can manage to come by it. 2. Ankar bāchha chāṭaī gāë, bhari muṅh bār hāthai āë Bhoj. Prov. If a cow another's calf should lick no gain is hers save hairs that stick. 3. Kesrī kes, pataṅg maṛīṅ, satī kuch, gaj dant, Sūr kaṭārī, bipr dhan, mare pailāge hast . The lion's mane, the jewel in snake's head, The Sati's breasts, ivory in elephant bred, The brave man's sword, the Brahman's wealth hoarded, No hand can ever touch till they are dead. 4. Sākh gaë phir hāth na āë . Prov. No credit lost is e'er regained. 2. To gain; accrue ( hāsil h ). Bair rakhe jo sā dhū sāth kachhu na āve vāke hāth . Prov. Who show t'wards saints hostility no gain their hands will e'er come by. hāth ūṅchā h , v. n. To be able to give; to be well off. hāth bāṅdhnā , v. n. To join the hands in supplication. hāth bāṅdhe khaṛe rahnā, hāth bāṅdhe rahnā , v. n. To wait upon; to attend. hāth baṛhānā, yā chalānā ; Sant. tī tīar , v. n. 1. To stretch one's hands towards. 2. To hand or pass anything. 3. ( hāth dauṛānā ) To make a long arm. 4. To exceed bounds; encroach upon. hāth bikānā , v. n. To be sold or made over to. Jog kīyā, par jugatī na jānā, Jam Rājā ke hāth bikānā . Kabir. hāth band yā taṅg h. , v. n. To be straitened or poor. hāth bharnā , v. n. 1. To be fatigued (the hands). 2. To be daubed or smeared (one's hands). hāth baiṭhnā yā jamnā , v. n. 1. To be set or used to; to be settled (one's hand); to have one's hand in. 2. To be driven home; to be effective. hāth pānī lenā , v. n. See āb-dast k . hāth pāoṅ bachānā , v. n. To protect oneself; to look out. hāth pāoṅ phūlnā , v. n. lit . to be swelled (the hands and feet). To be confounded. hāth pāoṅ tharrānā , v. n. To tremble or shake all over. hāth pāoṅ toṛnā , v. n. To maim. hāth pāoṅ ṭūṭnā , v. a. 1. To be broken (hands and feet). 2. To ache (the joints). hāth pāoṅ ṭhanḍe h . v. n. To become cold (the hands and feet). hāth pāoṅ chalnā , v. a. To be able-bodied, or able to work. Itnā to piṭ līyā, magar hāth pāoṅ chale hī jāte haiṅ! He has had such a beating, yet his hands and feet still go! hāth pāoṅ se chhuṭnā , Wom. v. n. To have a safe delivery. hāth pāoṅ mārnā, yā hilānā , v. a. 1. To throw out or work one's hands and legs. 2. To strike out (in swimming). 3. To throw one's arms about and jump in agony. 4. To strive hard; to struggle. 5. To work; labor. 1. Hāth pāoṅ hilā! Bhagvān degā . Prov. Stir your arms and legs and God will give. 2. Be-hāth pāoṅ hilāë kuchh nahīṅ hotā . Prov. Nothing can be done without stirring one's hands and feet. hāth pāoṅ nikālnā , v. a. 1. To be well-developed (the body). 2. To begin to show off. 3. To begin one's pranks. hāth pāoṅ hārnā , v. n. To lose courage; to be dispirited. hāth patthar tale dabnā , v. n. 1. To have the hands crushed under a stone. 2. To be entangled in a difficulty. 3. To be helpless, or unable to act. 4. To be brought to a dead stop. hāth par Qurān, yā Gaṅgā jalī rakhnā , v. a. To make one swear by the Quran or on Ganges' water; administer an oath. hāth par nāg khilānā , v. a. To risk one's life. Parāë bachchoṅ kā pālnā hāth pe nāg khilānā hai . To bring up another's child is to feed a serpent. hāth par hāth dhare baiṭhnā yā baiṭhe rahnā , To sit with folded hands; to do nothing. hāth par hāth mārnā , v. n. 1. To join hands in token of confirming a promise; to make a promise. 1. Bachan do, aur hāth pe hāth māro! Give me your word and strike your hands together! 2. Tab mār-kar uske hāth par hāth, Bolā ki hai qaul jān ke sāth . Nasīm. 2. To bet; pledge oneself. hāth paṛnā , v. n. To be plundered, looted. Āj bāzār meṅ hāth paṛ gayā. hāth pasārnā, yā phailānā , v. n. 1. To stretch out one's hands. Jahāṅ chār ādmī baiṭhe dekhe hāth phailā diye . Wherever four men sit hands (of beggars) stretched forth are. 2. To beg. hāth pasāre jānā , v. n. To go from this world empty-handed. Māl jinhoṅ ne jamā kīyā voh hāth pasāre jāte haiṅ . Who wealth have stored on earth with hands outstretched have gone out just. hāth pakaṛnā , v. n. 1. To take by the hands. 2. To take in hand or protect one. hāth phernā , v. n. 1. To stroke; caress. 2. To fleece; rob; ease of one's money. hāth pheṅknā , To brandish; wield (a sword, etc.). hāth pīle k. , Hin. v. n. lit . to stain the hands with turmeric. To get one married; have a quiet or inexpensive wedding. hāth taknā , v. n. 1. To look wistfully at one's hand. 2. To depend upon another for subsistence. hāth tale , Under one's hand. hāth thirkānā, yā nachānā , v. n. To dance one's hands; to gesticulate. hāth joṛnā ; Sant. joṛ hāt , v. a. 1. To join both hands (in supplication). 1. Kar gujrān garībi se, abhimānā kis par kartā hai? Kabīr. Jogī ho-ke jaṭā rakhāī, naṅge pāoṅ phirtā hai, Uṅchī bhujā kar hāth joṛtā, jo kyā Ishvar aṅdhā hai? Walk thou with true humility, For what hath pride to do with thee? Bare-foot, hair-matted hanging low, Doth God delight, is God served so? Why lift your hands both joined so high? Is God then blind, not ever nigh? 2. Koṛī nā māne hāthā-joṛī . Rus. The bribe taker will have you pay, beg hard with hands joined as you may. 2. To pay reverence or homage to. 3. To hold in fear or awe. Bas! tum ko hāth joṛtā hūṅ! Ironic. hāth jhāṛnā , v. n. 1. To give one a beating. 2. To strike a blow. Itnā sun kuṅjṛā uṭh dauṛā, līye hāth meṅ lāṭhī, Kuṅjṛan bhī dauṛī jhat se, līye hāth lukāṭhī, Hāth kuṅjṛe par jhāṛā . Kuṅjṛan kī laṛāī. hāth jhāṛ-ke khaṛā ho jānā , v. n. To stand up empty-handed; to empty one's purse. hāth jhaṭaknā , v. a. To draw away one's hand with a jerk. hāth jhūṭā k. , v. a. See ulush k . hāth jhūṭhā h. , v. n. 1. To be rendered unserviceable (one's hand). 2. To be smeared or dirty (one's hand with food). hāth-chālāk ; Illit. hath-chalāk, hath-chalā ; Sant. hāth-chuṭī , adj. 1. Light-fingered. 2. Dextrous; deft; nimble; skilful. hāth-chālākī , n. f. 1. Dexterity; sleight of hand; expertness. 2. Thievishness. hāth chalānā , v. a. 1. To use one's hands freely; to strike; beat. 2. To pass one's hands over. Āṅkheṅ parī-rukhoṅ se laṛātī haiṅ roṭiyāṅ, Sīnah upar bhī hāth chalāti haiṅ roṭiyāṅ . Nazīr. When bread their stomachs fills at fair ones they make eyes, And bread-fed lust on breasts its fingers plies. hāth chūmnā , v. n. To kiss one's hand reverentially. hāth chhoṛnā , v. a. To give a sword cut; to strike. hāth-khālī , adj. Empty-handed; penniless. hāth khālī nah., hāth kām meṅh . To have one's hand in; to be engaged; to have no leisure. hāth khālī nahīṅ hai . (Said to beggars). hāth khālī jānā , To have no picture card in a hand. hāth dikhānā , v. a. 1. To show one's palm (to a fortune-teller). 2. To show one's pulse to a physician. hāth dharnā, yā rakhnā , v. a. 1. To take by the hand. 2. To support; maintain; patronize; protect. hāth dho-ke pīchhe paṛnā , v. n. 1. To pursue closely in spite of every thing. 2. To persecute. hāth dhonā, hāth dho baiṭhnā , v. n. 1. ( hāth dhonā ) To wash one's hands. 2. To wash one's hands of. 3. To be despondent. Ham to apnī jān se hāth dho baiṭhe . hāth dekhnā , v. a. 1. To feel one's pulse. 2. To practise palmistry; tell one's fortunes. hāth denā , v. a. 1. To give one's hand to. 2. ( Horse-dealers and jewellers ) To make a secret bargain with the hands under a cloth. 3. To pledge oneself to. 4. To support a cause. 5. To put out a light. 6. See bāg moṛnā , 2. Mātā rānī hāth de gaī . 7. To as certain if one is possessed by an evil spirit by feeling his hand. 8. To exorcise. hāth ḍālnā , v. a. 1. ( hāth lagānā ) To thrust or put one's hands in. Khālā kī mehmānī, hāth ḍāl pachhtānī . Prov. Your aunt's hospitality's patent, put your hand in her dish and repent. 2. To touch or insult a woman. 3. To meddle; interfere in. 4. To plunder. hāth-ḍanḍī , n. f. A pair of hand scales. hāth roknā , 1. To hold or stay one's hand. 2. ( hāth sukeṛnā yā sameṭnā ) To keep back; withhold. 3. ( hāth kheṅchnā ) To use sparingly. hāth raṅgnā , v. n. 1. To dye one's hands with henna. 2. To be greased (one's palm); take bribes. hāth sādhnā, yā sāf k . v. a. To form one's hand. hāth sir par rakhnā , v. a. 1. To take under one's protection; to patronize. 2. To swear by one's head. hāth se . By the hands of; by; through. hāth se nikalnā , v. a. 1. To pass through one's hands. 2. To slip through one's fingers. hāth se de baiṭhnā, yā denā , v. a. To give away; bestow. Ātā ho to hāth se na dīje, Jātā ho to uskā gam na kīje! Prov. What comes to you let not your hands let go, Let not what goes from you your heart make low! hāth se gayā , adj. Lost; flown. hāth sāf k. , v. n. 1. To beat; trounce. 2. To cut one down. 3. To slay. 4. To fleece; plunder. 5. To practise hand-writing. hāth kāṭ denā , v. a. 1. To cut off one's hand. 2. To pledge or bind oneself. hāth kā jhūṭā . An unreliable or dishonest person. hāth-kā-dīyā . A gift; donation. Hāth kā dīyā sāth chale . Prov. Whate'er your hands have given will go with you to Heaven. hāth kā sachchā , adj. Honest; trustworthy. hāth kā mail , n. f. Vile trash; filthy lucre. Rupayā, paisā hāth kā mail hai. hāth kāṅpnā, yā tharrānā , v. n. To tremble (one's hand). hāth kaṭnā , v. n. 1. To be cut off (one's hand). 2. To be bound to. hāth k. , v. a. To cut at one ( wār k ). hāth khujlānā , v. n. To itch (the hands, to beat one). hāth kholnā , v. a. To be open-handed. hath kī chiṭṭhī , n. f. 1. A letter under one's own hand. 2. A receipt. hāth kī lakīreṅ ; Sant. tiol . 1. The lines on the palms of one's hands. 2. Fate. hāth ke nīche ānā, hāth tale ānā , v. n. To come under one's clutches. hāth-gāṛī , n. f. A hand-barrow. hāth-lapak . Light-fingered gentry. hāth lagānā , v. a. 1. To touch ( chhūnā , 1). Dekhat kī ek bhūrī bhārī, hāth lagāvat hove kārī . Riddle. Ans. Missī . To sight it's brown, touch it it's black. 2. To lay one's hands on. 1. Mere bachche ko hāth lagāyā to tum hī jāno! If you lay hands on my child, look out! 2. Jo tauṅh hāṅs ghaṛāve nāhīṅ tau tohe saṅg na soūṅgī, Khet kār-ke hāth nā lāūṅ, nā terī ṛoṭī poūṅgī . Rus. Wom. song. If you a necklace wont give me your bed I will not share, A bit of field-work I'll do ne'er, nor food for you prepare. 3. To put one's hand to; to begin (a work). hāth lagnā , v. n. 1. To come to hand ( hāth ānā ). 2. ( Arith .) To carry. Pandrah ke pāṅch hāth lagā ek. hāth lage mailā h . To be so pure and bright that the slightest touch would soil it. hāth mārnā , v. a. 1. See hāth sāf k . 3, 4, and hāth par hāth mārnā (1), 2. To pilfer; purloin; embezzle. 3. To play a good knife and fork. 4. To swallow large morsels quickly. hāth milānā , v. a. 1. To join or shake hands. 2. To join hands before wrestling. 3. To fight (hand with hand). 4. ( Shopkeepers ) To make a purchase. 5. To give alms. Āj ikādshī hai, kuchh hāth se hāth milāo! Brahmins. hāth malnā , v. n. 1. To wring one's hands. 1. Supne meṅ suāmī āë, kah na sakī ek bāt, Sovat thī, rovat uṭhī, mal mal rah gaī hāth! My spouse came to me in a dream, I naught to him could say, I slept all through, I weeping woke, my hands I wringing lay. 2. Yeh woh zamīṅ thī zamīṅ jiskī zar ugaltī thī, Yeh khāk voh thī kī aksīr hāth maltī thī , Fugān-i-Dehlī. Zahīr. A country this in history famed, whose soil once belched up gold, Its dust gold-dust which alchemists with envy did behold. hāth meṅ ṭhīkrā denā , v. a. To reduce one to beggary. hāth meṅ ṭhīkrā lenā , v. n. To take to begging; to beg. Aisā meṅh barsegā ki ṭhīkrā hāth meṅ līye phiroge . Soothsayers. It will rain so that you will go about with a shard in your hand. (1. You will have to bale out the water. 2. You will become a beggar). hāth meṅ dil rakhnā , v. a. To have a regard for one; to gratify every wish. hāth meṅ lānā , v. n. 1. To bring into one's hand. Hāth meṅ lānā, pāt meṅ khānā . Prov. Come to your hand, eaten on a leaf. (To live from hand to mouth). 2. To bring under. hāth meṅ lenā , v. a. To take in one hands; grasp; hold. hāth meṅ līye phirnā , v. n. To be randy. Phr. To carry one's J. T. in his hand. hāth meṅ hāth denā , v. a. To give one's hand in marriage. Tumhārā bāp aisā nādān nahīṅ ki tumhārā hāth ek gair ādmī ke hāth meṅ de de . 2. To hand over; give in one's charge. 3. To take one's hand to put one's hand in another's. hāth mei hunar h. , v. n. To be skilled in any handicraft or art. Jiske hāth meṅ hunar hai voh bhūkā nahīṅ maregā . Whose skill in handicraft doth lie of lunger he will never die. hāth na rakhne denā, puṭṭhe par hāth na dharne denā , v. n. 1. To shrink from the touch. 2. To be firm or unyielding. Id ā jo sirā hai to koī dukān-dār hāth nahīṅ charne detā . Bebrs Id prices rise so much there's not a thing you can e'en touch. 3. To be high-mettled, spirited (a horse). hāth hilīte, pallā jhulāte ānā , v. n. To come sweeping along without a penny to one's name. Kaṛī na lāë, hāth hilāte pallā jhulāte chale āë . Not a farthing he has brought, swinging his arms and flapping his sides he strides for naught. hāth hilië jānā , v. n. To go on eating. hāthoṅ chāoṅ rakhnā, yā k. , v. a. To ake under one's wing. hāthoṅ senikalnā , v. n. 1. To pass through one's hands. 2. To escape from one's control. hāthoṅ kaejah uchhalnā , v. n. To palpitate (the heart). hāthoṅ mei rakhnā , v. a. To bring up tenderly. hāthoṅ hāt , adv. 1. From hand to hand; out of hand. Harī raābī, chiṭṭā bhāt, Lo re pacho hāthoṅ hāth! Riddle. Ans. Chambelī . Jasmine. 2. Quiclly; expeditiously. hāthoṅ hāth ur jānā, yā biknā , v. n. To find a ready sale. hāthoṅ hāth lejānā , v. n. To carry away quickly. hāthoṅ hāth lenā , To receive one with respect. apne ūpar hāth chalānā , v. n. To lay violent hands upon oneself; to attempt or commit suicide. ulṭā hāth mārnā , v. a. To give one a back-hander. donoṅ hāthoṅ sameṭnā , v. a. lit . To collect with both hands. To coin money. kālak ke hāth lagānā , v. a. To bring disgrace on one. kisī ke hāth bhejnā , v. a. To send by some one. kisī ke hāth bechnā , v. a. To sell to some one.

Cont. of ﮩـاتهـ q. v. hath-udhār . See dast-gardāṅ , 1. hath udhār, yā hath pher denā . To lend for use. hath-pher , n. f. 1. Sleight of hand (in a money changer); changing a good for a bad rupee. 2. E. Borrowing. 3. Fondling; caress. hath-pher k. , v. a. 1. To caress. 2. To embezzle. 3. To gulp down. hath pher lenā , E. v. n. To borrow. hath-chhuṭ ; Brij, hath chhuṭkū , One who is in the habit of striking others. hath-ras , n. 1. Amorous dalliance. 2. Masturbation. Hai hāth kī garmī meṅ jo az-bas kī bharā ras, Is wāste kahte haiṅ sab is kām ko hath-ras . Nazīr. hath-kaṭī , n. f. A sword-cut which is intended to take off or disable the adversary's hand. hath-kaṛī , n. f. A handcuff; manacle. hath-khaṅḍe , n. m. 1. Manual dexterity. 2. Legerdemain. hath-levā , n. m. A part of the Hin. marriage ceremony. The hands of the bride and bridegroom are joined palm to palm with some flour put between them, and tied with a red thread. hattoṅ se jānā , v. n. To cut and run. hat'thī, hatho , W. Rus. 1. A stick for stirring up the juice of the sugarcane. 2. A hair glove for cleaning horses. hatte chaṛhnā , v. n. To come under one's power. hathyā'na, hatyānā , v. n. To appropriate; nab; obtain by fraud. Ser chūn maṅg kanglā lāyā, chhīn paṛausan ne hatyāyā . Rus. Prov. A seer of flour a pauper begged and brought, a neighbour grabbed and left her naught. (One does the work, another draws the pay). hath'lī, hathrī, haithī , n. f. The winch or handle of a spinning-wheel. hatauṭ'ī , n. f. 1. Mode of handling or wielding. 2. Skill; dexterity. hat'thā , A large bunch (of plantains). Ek hatthā kelā aur thorā sā thakūā maiṅ ne chhaṭh meṅ buā ko bhejā hai . Tir. hatho'nā, hathaunā , Sweet cakes put in the hands of the bride and bridegroom.

s.m. The hand; arm; — paw; trunk (of an elephant); — a cubit (measure); — a hand (in an office, or a factory, &c.); — a hand (at cards); — a blow, slap; — (met.) reach; possession, clutches; power; protection, patronage; — adv. By the hands (of, kisī-ke), by, through; — to (e.g. kisī-ke hāth de-denā or beć-denā, 'to make over to, or to sell to, any one)
more: s. m. 1. The hand. 2. A cubit. 3. Possession, power. هاتهہ آنا hāth ānā, To come into oneʼs possession or power, to be gained or obtained. هاتهہ اٿهانا hāth uṭhānā, 1. To leave off, refrain from, desist, abandon, relinquish, resign, cease. 2. To salute by raising the hand to the head. Both these meanings are alluded to in the following example. Muṣt̤afạ̄ ḵẖān Yakrang says: هاتهہ اٿها جوراورجفا سي تو يہي گويا سلام هي تيرا Hāth uṭhā jaur aur jafā se tū; yih-ī goyā salām hai terā. Restrain thy hands from violence and injustice; this is thy salutation. 3. To beat. 4. To give alms. هاتهہ اوٿ لينا hāth oṭ lenā, To receive with both hands open and stretched out together. هاتهہ باندهنا hāth-bāndhnā, To join the hands in a supplicating posture. هاتهہ بڙهانا hāth baṛhānā, 1. To endeavour to get any thing. 2. To gain possession of the property of others. هاتهہ بند هونا hāth band honā, 1. To be much…
subs: v.n. To come to hand, to reach, be received; to come into the possession, or power (of, -ke, or mere, &c.); to fall (to); to be gained, be obtained, be found
v.n. The hand to be dislocated
(-ko), To give with the hand; — to give reluctantly
To raise the hand or hands (in salutation, or prayer, &c.), to salute; to pray (for); to give alms; — to raise the hand (against, -par), to strike, beat

(-se), To withdraw the hand (from); to keep the hands off; to leave off, cease, desist (from), refrain (from), keep (from); to abandon, forego, relinquish, renounce, resign; — to release, let go, set at liberty (i.q. P. dast bar-dāshtan); — to give up hope (of), to despair (i.q. hāth dhonā)
v.t. To take or receive with both hands open and stretched out together
v.n. 'The hand (of a person) to be raised'; to be in a position (or to be able) to give; to be well-off
To join the hands in a supplicating posture
v.n. 'To remain standing with hands joined,' to continue standing in attendance (on), to wait upon

To stretch out the hand (towards, or for); to endeavour to get (anything); to hand or pass (a thing); — to gain possession of the property (of another); to encroach (upon), to exceed bounds (i.q. hāth dauṛānā)
To be sold or made over (to)
'The hands (of a person) to be tied'; to be in straitened circumstances, to be poor or helpless; — to be much engaged in business, to have no leisure
v.n. The hands to become wearied or fatigued; — the hands to be covered or stained (or daubed or smeared, by or with)
or baiṭh-jānā, v.n. The hand to be set or used (to); to have the hand in practice, to acquire perfection by practice (in any art, &c.); — to be driven home, to be effective
v.t. To sell or dispose of (to another)
or hāth-pāṅwoṅ, or hāth-pāʼoṅ, s.m. Hand and foot; hands and feet; — a right-hand man, a factotum
(-ke), To tie the hands and feet (of), to bind (one) hand and foot
To protect oneself, to look out
The hands and feet to be or become swollen; — to be or become distressed or confounded
To extend (one's) business or schemes; — to obtain (things) fraudulently; to take bribes
The hands and legs to tremble; to tremble or shake all over
(-ke), To break the hands and feet (of); to maim; to cripple
The hands and feet to break or be broken; — to suffer breaking pains in the limbs (as in incipient fever, &c.)
v.n. The limbs to be movable or active; to be able-bodied, to be able to work
To wash the hands and feet; — to go to the necessary
To get off safe and sound; — to have a safe delivery

To throw about the hands and legs; to work the hands and legs; to sprawl; to flounder; — to strike out (in swimming); — to throw the arms about and jump about (in agony); — to be agitated; — to strive hard, struggle, endeavour, strain, toil, labour

'To put forth the hands and feet'; to begin to show off; — to begin one's pranks; to pursue a loose (or an evil) course of conduct; to show one's teeth; — the limbs (or the body) to become well-developed
v.n. To lose courage, to become dispirited
'The hand to be crushed under a stone'; to be entangled in a difficulty; — to be helpless, to be unable to act; — to be brought to a dead stop
adv. On the hand; — on the nail
To be placed on the hand (ready for another to take); to be in readiness (for another to take or accept)
(or gaṅgā-jalī) rakhnā, 'To put the Qorʼān (or Ganges-water) in the hands' (of anyone, to swear by); to make one swear by the Qorʼān, &c.; to administer an oath (to)
'To feed a serpent on (one's) hand'; to place life in jeopardy, to risk life
hāth-par hāth-dhare baiṭhnā or baiṭhe rahnā

n. m. S. हस्त The 13th mansion of the moon; Corvus. 1. Hathiyā barse tīn hot haiṅ, shakkar, shālī, māsh, Hathiyā barse tīn jāt haiṅ, tillī, kodo, kapās . When in the thirteenth mansion rain doth fall Rice, sugarcane, and pulse, these three thrive all, But cotton, kodo , sesamum, all three The rains destroy beyond all remedy. 2. Chaṛhte barse ardarā, utrat barse hast, Kitnau Rājā ḍāṅṛ le rahe anand girkast . If in the sixth rain falls and in the thirteenth mansion too, Whate'er the king may take the peasantry are well-to-do. 3. Hathiyā barse, chitrā maṅḍrāë, ghar baiṭhe kisān ririyāë . Bhoj. Prov. Rains in the thirteenth mansiou, and the next with clouds o'erhung, The peasantry sit in their homes with grief and sorrow wrung.
more: n. f. S. हन् kill. 1. Slaughter; murder. 2. A sin. 1. Chhoṭī sī bachhiyā baṛī sī hattyā . Prov. The calf killed is so small, the sin so great withal. 2. Khassī mār gharvaiyā khāë, hattiyā le le pāhun jāë . Prov. The household all dine on the goat that's slain, The sin of slaughter on the guest's head lain. 3. Plague; pest. Udhār baṛī hattyā hai . Prov. Borrowing is a plague. 4. A very weak or sick person. Is hattyā se kyā laṛūṅ? 5. A quarrel. Hattyā kā mūl hai . hattyā palle baṅdhnā, yā mol lenā , v. n. To be nailed to a cross. hattyā k. , v. a. 1. To kill; murder. 2. To commit a sin.
